content: The Mound Next Door
A story by:
credit:
Kayla Payne, Ashim F. Talukdar, Victor Morgan.
1 June. 2026

content: A story about a hill that's not a hill, the Indigenous people who lived here long before us, and one of Ohio's greatest archaeological treasures hiding in plain sight.
Ohio University

Between Athens and The Plains stands a 40-foot mound made of earth that has survived centuries of change. It has endured farming, road construction, and the passage of time. Known as Hartman Mound, it is the best-preserved remaining mound of the Wolfe Plains Group, but it is far from the only one.

Built over generations, each phase of construction began with ceremonial deposits and wooden mortuary structures, which were subsequently covered beneath thick strata of carefully placed earth.
Map.png
credit: Of 30 earthworks that were discovered in the 1800s, only a few remain.

content: Roughly 2,800 years ago, while the Roman Republic was still taking shape across the Atlantic, the Adena people were leaving their mark on what is now Ohio. They constructed impressive mounds and earthworks, maintained long-distance trade networks that reached the Gulf of Mexico, and developed traditions that continue to capture the attention of archaeologists today.
Time Warp
WARNING: You are about to enter the Time Machine...

To fully understand the modern landscape of Athens County, the timeline must be traced back to its earliest structural origins. Navigating through the historical strata of the Wolfe Plains complex reveals how a modern community was built directly over a vast, ancient ceremonial network. Let us examine the first era of this landscape.
Ready? Let's go back.
ERA 1 — 800 BCE to 100 CE
Before Ohio Had its Name

The Adena people did not arrive in what is now Athens County by accident. They selected this landscape with purpose, transforming the Wolfe Plains into a carefully designed ceremonial center. Here, burial mounds, sacred enclosures, and gathering spaces formed a place where communities came together to honor their ancestors, conduct rituals, and mark important moments in life and death.

Among these earthworks stood the Hartman Mound, one of the largest mounds in the Wolfe Plains group. Its construction was neither quick nor simple. Generation after generation, the mound grew through a deliberate process: ceremonial offerings were placed, wooden structures were erected for burials, and layers of earth were carefully added above them. Each layer represented another chapter in a tradition that endured for centuries.

Adena necklaces have been found made of Marginella shells sourced from the Atlantic Ocean or the Gulf of Mexico.

The Adena had trade networks stretching hundreds of miles.

This reconstruction shows what an Adena man may have looked like based on archaeological evidence. Because the Adena left no written records, their individual names are unknown today.

Using archaeological discoveries, he will help us explore how the Adena may have built the remarkable mounds and earthworks that still exist today.

Archaeological records indicate that leadership and spiritual roles within Adena society were deeply tied to lineage and visual representation.

Physical regalia, including hairstyles and physical adornments, designated status during pan-regional communal assemblies.

Through generations, these distinctive practices preserved historical lineage across the Ohio River Valley basin.

Today, the mound remains as evidence of a society whose vision, engineering, and spiritual traditions shaped the landscape long before written history reached Ohio.

Hartman Mound, now the most prominent surviving mound in the group, was among the largest constructed by the Adena people. It was built in successive layers over many generations, with ceremonial deposits and wooden burial structures carefully placed before additional earth was added. This process was repeated over time, gradually creating the impressive mound that remains today.
ERA 2 — Early 1800s
New Neighbors Arrive
Settlers saw flat land and thought: farmable.
The Adena saw the same land and thought: sacred.

When Euro-American settlers moved into the Ohio River Valley in the early 1800s, the Wolfe Plains area began to change fast. The wide, flat plains that made these lands ideal for Adena ceremonial activity also made them perfect for agriculture.

Many of the smaller mounds, the enclosures, the satellite earthworks, the ceremonial rings, did not survive the 19th century. They were plowed under, quietly, without anyone really noticing what was being lost.
Hartman Mound survived. Partly because it was big, 40 feet tall, and difficult to plow through. Partly luck. The Wolfe Plains Group originally included 13 identified mound and earthwork sites. Today, most are no longer visible above ground.

The same open plains that made Wolfe Plains a perfect ceremonial gathering spot — visible, accessible, central — made it a prime target for agricultural clearing.

The land worked for the Adena's purposes, and later the settlers used the same land for farming.
ERA 3 — Early 1900s
William C. Mills Shows Up

Enter William Corless Mills, archaeologist and curator at the Ohio State Archaeological and Historical Society, and arguably the reason we know as much as we do about Wolfe Plains.

In the early 1900s, Mills conducted systematic surveys and excavations of Adena earthwork sites across Ohio. He documented mound structures, map layout design structures, and recorded burial contents through field notes and excavation reports. His work on Wolfe Plains is one of the earliest formal records of the site.
The artifacts he and other early archaeologists uncovered — pottery, copper ornaments, carved stone pipes, ornamental objects — opened a window into Adena trade networks and artistic traditions that would otherwise be invisible to us today.

But there is a complicated side to this story. Excavation methods in the early 1900s were not exactly gentle. Mound interiors were disturbed, burial contexts were lost, and valuable information disappeared in the process. By modern standards, some of what was done to learn about the past also erased parts of it.

Early twentieth century surveys painstakingly cataloged the remaining earthworks, creating permanent records of monuments that faced imminent destruction from modern expansion.

These recovered physical objects provided academic circles with the first definitive proof of a highly sophisticated, regional trade ecosystem.

The Adena Pipe: Among the most iconic Adena artifacts ever found is the Adena Pipe; a carved effigy pipe depicting a human figure dressed only in a small loincloth. It was likely used in ritual tobacco ceremonies.
Pipe.png
Stone Tablets: Used for stamping tools used to press patterns onto textiles, animal skins, and possibly human skin during ritual ceremonies.
ERA 4 — Mid-to-Late 1900s
Roads, Suburbs, and Mound Loss
Do you ever look at a place and think, 'There has to be a story here'? Welcome to that feeling.

The mid-20th century brought roads, suburban expansion, and the full transformation of The Plains into the community it is today. US Route 33 carved through the region. Residential streets were laid. The Wolfe Plains Group which had already lost most of its outer earthworks to 19th-century farming now had to contend with active construction.

Several mound sites were built over. Others were left technically intact but practically invisible, buried under lawns, sitting in private backyards, stranded between roads. Fragmented and disconnected from their original spatial relationships with each other.

The Four Main Causes of Loss:

1. Agriculture: Earliest and most widespread damage, many were leveled.
2. Development: The biggest modern threat, structures built directly over sites.
3. Roads: Fragmented the site, severing mound-to-mound relationships.
4. Erosion: Slow, ongoing and gradually reshaping what remains.

Hartman Mound is 40 feet tall and fully visible in The Plains. You can go see it. Today. For free.

Mid-century infrastructure expansion permanently disrupted the historic pathways that once unified these remote earthworks.

The Folklore Rabbit Hole: Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, folklore and conspiracy theories circulated claiming the mounds held the remains of giants. Newspapers ran wild with it. The myth was frequently leveraged by nineteenth-century writers to minimize or deny the architectural achievements of local Indigenous civilizations.
ERA 5 — Today
Against all odds, the mounds are still here. Turns out 2,800 years of stubbornness is hard to argue with.

Most of the Wolfe Plains Group's original 13 sites are no longer visible above ground, but some survived. Hartman Mound, standing 40 feet tall sitting in The Plains is the most visible reminder that this neighborhood has a story that goes back way before any of the houses or streets around it. Today, the site is documented, studied, and increasingly protected, thanks to local advocates, archaeologists, and organizations like Friends of the Plains Mound. What we've lost matters. What we still have matters more.
Very few mounds remain fully intact above ground.
Culture.png
Why Should You Care?
Highlighting History

Three Reasons to Care:

A Bigger Story: The Adena people were not just local, their trade networks stretched across half the continent. They possessed shells from the Gulf of Mexico and copper from the Great Lakes. Overall, their presence here was substantial.

Adena Legacy: The Friends of the Plains Mounds in Athens County, Ohio is a non-profit dedicated to promoting the awareness and protection of the ancient Adena mounds, earthworks, and Indigenous heritage of The Plains. They need people like you to know it exists.
Learn more

Follow Friends of the Plains Mound on Facebook for updates, events, and photographs of the mound. You can also reach them at friendsoftheplains@gmail.com or by mail at P.O. Box 723, Athens, OH 45701.

The earthworks stood long before the modern settlement pattern took shape, remaining as a permanent physical testament to the region's deep historical landscape.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JegXnhDo2oM

Speaker Alex Armstrong, President of the Friends of the Plains Mounds, explains the Native American burial mounds in The Plains, Ohio, and the surrounding areas. Armstrong provides insight into the ceremonial life and social structure of the early Indigenous peoples of the Athens County area through his presentation.

Sources: Ohio Archaeology (Bradley T. Lepper), Athens County Historical Society Archives, Archaeological Survey Reports of Ohio Earthworks, OhioArchaeology.org, openvirtualworlds.org, ohioslargestplayground.com
